health_related_information.health_related_information_details_line_: Ethan has mild, well-controlled asthma that is primarily exercise- and cold-induced. He uses a blue Ventolin (salbutamol) inhaler as needed, typically before vigorous physical activity or if he starts to wheeze. His last asthma flare-up requiring an ER visit was in March 2022; none since then, and he has never been hospitalized overnight for asthma.

He also has a diagnosed tree nut allergy (especially walnuts and pecans). We carry an EpiPen Jr. for him at all times. Please do NOT allow him to share food with other children, and he should avoid baked goods or snacks that are not clearly labeled as nut-free. If he shows signs of an allergic reaction (hives, swelling around the lips or eyes, difficulty breathing, or vomiting), please administer the EpiPen and call 911 immediately, then contact me or his father right away.

Ethan wears prescription glasses for nearsightedness; he sometimes takes them off during sports and may misplace them. If he complains of not seeing the board or screen clearly, it usually just means his glasses are off or smudged. He is generally healthy and active, enjoys soccer and drawing, and has no physical limitations with running, jumping, or participating in regular activities.

There have been some recent family changes: his father and I separated amicably in late 2023, and Ethan now lives primarily with me but sees his dad every other weekend and one evening a week. He may occasionally seem a bit quiet or sensitive around pick-up and transition times, especially on Mondays after a weekend with his dad. If you notice him becoming withdrawn, tearful, or unusually frustrated, a quick check-in, a quiet space, or offering him a drawing activity usually helps him calm down. He responds well to clear, calm instructions and advance notice before transitions (e.g., “In 5 minutes we’re going to clean up and head inside”).
